data.title: What Bond Market Volatility Says About The Economy And Corporate Earnings
data.description:
It’s been a volatile stretch for bond markets recently, as investors deal with a multitude of crosscurrents, including tariffs and economic uncertainty. Alexandra Gorewicz, Vice President & Director, Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management of TD Asset Management, speaks with MoneyTalk’s Kim Parlee about what investors should be focusing on in the shifting fixed income landscape.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - It has been a volatile stretch for global bond markets recently. In the US, the effect of a prolonged government shutdown has left its mark, with investors flying blind without federal data, while the Supreme Court questions the legality of tariffs.

Meanwhile, in Canada, the latest federal budget has been described as long on ambition but short on near-term growth. So with so many crosscurrents, how should investors make sense of what's happening in fixed income right now? Joining me now is Alex Gorewicz, Portfolio Manager, Active Fixed Income at TD Asset Management, to explain everything. That's a tall order.

Alexandra Gorewicz - It is, yes.

Kim Parlee - Go. No. Just explain it all.

Alexandra Gorewicz - No, I don't do that, Kim. Nice to see you.

Kim Parlee - Well, where do you -- it's always lovely to see you. Where do you want to start? When you think about the picture that we painted in courtesy of some of the things that you've been telling us of what you're watching, what are some of the more material things you're watching right now? And then we can dive into them.

Alexandra Gorewicz - I think at a high level, it's the totality of the data. I know some data is missing. But we still have a lot of information that we can process and that we are processing, whether it's government data at state levels, whether it's alternative private sector data, whether it's Federal Reserve data.

The Federal Reserve is independent of the government. And they're continuing to publish survey data and other good quality data for us to process. And when we look at the totality of that data, what it's telling us is that growth is chugging along, but it's neither very strong nor very weak. So from a bond market perspective, there are a lot of additional things that investors are paying attention to, namely earnings season and looking at -- we'll call it beats and misses of various sectors or stocks.

Kim Parlee - Let me ask -- so we'll focus on the US market first. Then we'll get to Canada second. But let's go on the corporate side a bit, so whether it's corporate bonds or just the corporate data that you're getting to understand what's going on. And I should mention, there's a lot of supply of corporate bonds coming out right now, and I think a lot of demand actually sucking all that supply up. But what are you seeing from the earnings and the misses? And what is that telling you?

Alexandra Gorewicz - I think the earnings and the misses aren't real misses. I think they're misses relative to expectations that earnings will beat 20%, 30% relative to expectations. Those superbeats aren't happening. We'll call it with the same frequency as they were a quarter or two ago. And I think it's causing some investors to take stock of the picture. And then in addition to that, that debt issuance you're talking about, and let's say specifically on AI-related debt issuance, that wasn't a factor a quarter or two ago. And now it is. And the moment debt joins the party, there are growing concerns that, oh, maybe this is very late-stage.

Kim Parlee - Yeah. Tell me more about that, just that late-stage piece. Why -- tell me like I'm six years old. Why does that matter? How does that change the picture?

Alexandra Gorewicz - Because some investors feel that in order to maintain the same level of growth prospects, you now need to fund it with debt. And that's absolutely not the case. If you look at some of these very large AI...

Kim Parlee - These are assets.

Alexandra Gorewicz - That's right. And they have so much cash on their balance sheet. But there's also so much demand for these bonds like you're talking about. You mentioned there's a lot of supply but a lot of demand that meets it. So, for example, I won't mention specific stocks, but the likes of a Google or a Meta, they've come to market, and demand for, let's say, a $10 or $15 billion issue has been closer to 100 billion. So it just gives you a sense of the magnitude of how much investors are actually excited about these investment opportunities, even if -- from a broader AI theme perspective, it feels a little frothy.

Kim Parlee - Interesting. Interesting. It's kind of like -- yeah. Is it the good or the bad? You're kind of just waiting to see. I guess it depends on your longer term and what you think is going to happen. I need to ask you, though, as well about -- I mentioned the Fed. I mentioned, of course, there's going to be somebody new coming in at some point, also the legality of the tariffs. And that has a huge impact on government bonds as well, too.

Alexandra Gorewicz - Yes. So a couple of things on that. When we think about the tariffs and the Supreme Court ruling, we won't really know which way they're going to rule. They have to consider all of the arguments that they've heard. It could take, I don't know, a month, two months. So expect that to be at some point December or January after the next Fed meeting.

But I think from a bond market perspective, investors are looking in this situation and saying, if the Supreme Court says the US administration isn't allowed to use IEEPA or the emergency powers to enact tariffs, well, there is $30 billion a month of revenue from these tariffs that the government doesn't have access to anymore. How will that be funded? And will that mean that the bond market is going to be faced with a lot more Treasury bond supply?

What we have noticed is the long end of the yield curve, so thinking about 30-year bonds and longer, those are very sensitive to supply dynamics. So any whiff of additional bond supply will very likely see those longer-term interest rates rise.

When it comes to the Fed, this idea of a new Fed Chair, the Fed's credibility or independence being compromised because President Trump will put somebody that sees eye to eye with him on monetary policy - none of this is really reflected in the bond market at the moment. Corporate spreads are very tight. Government bond yields are actually lower year-to-date across the entire yield curve. Inflation expectations are very anchored, even shorter term, longer term.

What we would be worried about if bond investors start to get worried that the Fed's actions are not really in line with economic developments would be if longer-term inflation expectations start to rise. And again, here you see this reinforcement of that pushing up long bond yields higher. So there are risks that the fallen long-term interest rates we've seen in the US year-to-date, that could reverse in the coming quarters.

Kim Parlee - I want to squeeze a couple more questions in. I mean, we've had -- and this is not in the public markets. In the private markets, we've heard about concerns about private credit and just some of those things. Is there any spillover that happens into the public markets from that?

Alexandra Gorewicz - I think I wouldn't characterize it as spillover. I would characterize it more like a confluence of factors that make investors question - we'll call it the health of the economic fundamentals relative to those tight corporate spreads which exist...

Kim Parlee - That frothiness, again, right?

Alexandra Gorewicz - That frothiness again. In some cases, absolutely justified. High-quality blue-chip companies, even if they're coming to market and issuing more debt, they still have tons of cash. Strong operating margins. No concerns there. But whether you're talking about private or public, the reality is interest rates are higher than where they were several years ago, and inflation is higher. That eats into margins. So costs are pressured. If revenues become pressured as well, for example, some public high-yield issuers, they tend to cater to lower-income consumers.

Lower-income consumers are finding it really, really challenging to deal with the high cost of living because even if year-on-year inflation isn't running away like it was back in 2022, price levels matter. And their wage growth is not keeping up with where prices are. So when you look at the totality of where some issuers, public or private, are operating, some of the weaker segments are absolutely struggling. And so in private markets in particular, you've had some wobbles, some either defaults or bankruptcies. And that has caused some angst in investors.

Kim Parlee - If we turn to Canada, we saw a big budget come out, generational, and it depends on your perspective in terms of whether you believe that to be true. But that's what it was called. A couple of questions I'll ask you. What are the two most important things you're watching coming out of this budget? And what bond investors are going to be paying attention to?

Alexandra Gorewicz - The first one would be the way I characterize the budget is not generational, short-term pain for long-term gain. Maybe not too much short-term pain, but what I mean by that is there are a lot of cost-cutting measures that should be felt relatively quickly, whether it's reducing the public labor force, whether it's cutting down on some programming costs, that could have an impact of reducing growth prospects in Canada in the near term.

All the capital investments are long-term. And in particular, the idea of trying to attract -- we'll call it manufacturing or industrial capital - to the extent you can do that, you should see that reflected in the first several quarters of next year, if not by the end of next year. If that doesn't happen, immediately, the bond market will say, okay, so growth should be a lot weaker in Canada.

The second thing that I am watching is those cost-cutting measures. If they don't materialize, we'll call it 60 billion that the government is trying to target that will probably need to be funded with debt. And again, the bond market will look at it and go lower growth prospects and higher debt, not a good combination.

Kim Parlee - Yeah, and the net on that will be what, when you look out of the combination of the investors not believing, perhaps, the prognosis of what the federal government is saying?

Alexandra Gorewicz - I think what that would do is it would force Bank of Canada to probably take more action by cutting interest rates further, which would pressure shorter-term bond yields lower, but longer-term bond yields would very likely respond to higher debt supply longer term, and that would very likely push up bond yields at the long end of the yield curve, so we would get what we call a steepening of the yield curve.

Kim Parlee - What about -- just going back to the first part you said there - and I've got about 90 seconds here - but when you think about -- you said you want to see signs that investment is coming into Canada, what are those signs? What will you look for to say that, yes, we're attracting investment as we had hoped to?

Alexandra Gorewicz - Well, business investment, first and foremost...

Kim Parlee - Just see it.

Alexandra Gorewicz - ...which has flatlined, we actually want to see it. I think before we even want to see net positive business investment, we just don't want to see capital going down south anymore, flying down south, if you will. Capital will go where it's best treated. And at the moment, it's best treated in the US. But hopefully, some of the changes that have come through in this budget in Canada would keep some of that money here at home. But now, we just need to see some positive dynamics as well. And I do believe that if the government doesn't see that, I do believe that they would take more steps this time next year in the next budget.

Kim Parlee - And something to incent. And the same thing I would say on the other side is, in terms of crystallizing those savings, to see that actually impacts the run rate.

Alexandra Gorewicz - Correct. So a lot of unknowns, but I think they've given themselves enough flexibility to be able to respond accordingly.

Original Post

data.title: Boise Cascade: Vertical Integration Is The Name Of The Game
data.description:
Company Overview

I continue my research of the forest industry with Boise Cascade (BCC), one of the largest manufacturers of plywood and engineered wood products in North America. In addition to the 11 plywood plants, 5 EWP facilities, and 2 sawmills included in its Wood Products segment, BCC also operates a network of 39 distribution centers across the continental United States. The two business lines are vertically integrated to a degree I had not seen in the industry before, and this is one of BCC's most important and durable competitive advantages. To gain a better understanding of the overall impact of vertical integration within the forest industry, I would like to draw some parallels between Boise Cascade and the Timberland REITs I have previously covered. As Michael Porter discussed in his famous book 'Competitive Strategy,' vertical integration can often improve bargaining power with clients and suppliers, thereby translating into enhanced profitability and returns on invested capital.

If buyers either are partially integrated or pose a credible threat of backward integration, they are in a position to demand bargaining concessions [...] Selective backward integration may lower costs and put pressure on competitors who cannot afford such integration.

Vertical Integration In The Forest Industry

In a previous article about PotlatchDeltic (PCH), I discussed the strategic advantages created by the vertical integration of timberland and manufacturing assets within the same portfolio. On the timberland side of the business, internally owned mills create a secure and stable source of log demand. In turn, vertical integration also provides a secure and abundant access to the raw materials needed by the manufacturing arm. The overall result is in the form of enhanced stability and resiliency relative to unintegrated peers, and this is particularly true during challenging market environments like we are in today.

The profitability of timberland assets is a lot more resilient and predictable relative to mills of any kind. This means that timberlands can act as a source of capital when the manufacturing segment needs it the most, creating counter-cyclical investment opportunities. Across a commodity cycle, having access to capital even in the midst of a severe industry downturn is key to generating compelling returns on invested capital.

To understand the challenges associated with an unintegrated manufacturing business in the midst of a cyclical downturn, one can look into West Fraser's adjusted EBITDA margin of -10% in Q3-25 compared to the adjusted EBITDA breakevens at which Weyerhaeuser's (WY) and PotlatchDeltic's manufacturing segments are currently operating.

If we come back to Boise Cascade, I can see how the distribution network of building materials acts a bit like timberland assets when it comes to damping the volatility of its manufacturing business. The key difference is that timberlands are the very first step in the forestry supply chains, while the distribution of finished materials is one of the last before homebuilders like D.R. Horton (DHI) or Lennar (LEN) take the lead. This is a great example of the differences between forward and backward vertical integration.

As illustrated below, BCC's EBITDA declined by 81% on a year-over-year basis in its Wood Products segment, while the profitability of the BMD segment declined by 20%. It still represents a sharp decline, but it explains why the distribution business now represents almost 85% of consolidated EBITDA before corporate eliminations.

With the vast majority of its manufacturing assets located in the Pacific Northwest and in the U.S. South, Boise Cascade is one of the largest customers of the three publicly listed timberland REITs previously covered, as it is also where most of their acreage is located. According to BCC's 2024 Annual Report, logs accounted for almost 80% of overall wood fiber costs, and approximately 90% of those purchases were made from private landowners or dealers.

The only thing missing for Boise Cascade to become fully integrated across the supply chain would be timberlands in the vicinity of its plywood and EWP mills. I do not think this is in the company's long-term plans to do so, but if we push the concept of vertical integration one step further, this is what it would look like: from timberlands to wholesale distribution of building materials. On the distribution side, it is interesting to note that Builders FirstSource (BLDR) and Home Depot (HD) are Boise Cascade's two largest customers.

For the full year of 2024, approximately 70% of BCC's Wood Products segment revenues were from internal sales to the BMD segment. More precisely, roughly 75% and 50% of EWP and plywood sales were to the BMD segment, respectively. Viewed from another perspective, substantially all the EWPs sold by the distribution segment are sourced internally from the manufacturing business. Boise Cascade has by far the highest level of vertical integration I am aware of in the forest industry. If you are aware of any other company in the industry with an even higher level of vertical integration, please let me know.

Balance Sheet & Capital Allocation

Boise Cascade ended the third quarter of 2025 with a cash balance of $511.8 million and $395.2 million in undrawn bank line availability, for total available liquidity of $907.0 million. In turn, the company had $445.1 million of long-term debt outstanding at the end of September 2025, leaving BCC with net debt of $66.7 million. This is obviously in the context of a capital-intensive industry, particularly on the manufacturing side, but I still view BCC's balance sheet and access to capital as another competitive advantage relative to smaller and unintegrated peers. Of note, S&P upgraded the company's credit rating from ''BB-'' to ''BB'' in April 2024, noting vertical integration as a key competitive advantage and margin enhancer.

As mentioned in the paragraph above, Boise Cascade ended the third quarter of 2025 with net debt of only $66.7 million. However, I think it is important to keep in mind that it is in the context of a capital-intensive industry. For the first nine months of 2025, Boise Cascade generated $123.1 million in operating cash flows, which is well below the $187.5 million in capital expenditures. In fact, the operating cash flows were not even enough to cover the ~$140 million deployed toward share repurchases and dividends over the same period. In such a situation, most of the capital expenditures must be funded from the balance sheet and not from operations.

The capital intensity of BCC's business lines is the key reason why its balance sheet deteriorated from a $267.1 million net cash position at the end of 2024 to a $66.7 million net debt position as of the end of Q3-25. The strength of Boise Cascade's balance sheet is not in question today, but what happens if the current downturn continues for a few more years, and what if it gets even worse? Yes, capital expenditures can probably be reduced even further if industry conditions worsen, but that would mean pretty much the same for the underlying profitability of the business. It is from this perspective that the balance sheet may become a more pressing issue down the road, as nobody knows when the next upcycle will come.

What About Mortgage Rates?

My 'Hold' rating primarily reflects my personal views about long-term interest rates in the United States. Like any other company operating within the forestry supply chain, BCC's end markets are also intimately connected with the state of the U.S. housing market. More specifically, housing starts and repair or remodeling activities are largely dependent upon credit conditions, and this is when long-term interest and mortgage rates come into play.

The United States reported a fiscal deficit of $1.8 trillion in fiscal 2025, which takes into account the $970 billion in interest expenses. Fiscal deficits above the $1.0 trillion mark have become something structural since the world-changing events of 2020, and I can only imagine what could happen when the next major crisis hits. Given the deteriorating geopolitical landscape all around the world, including in the Middle East or around Taiwan, could the next major crisis be a war? If it is the case, what happens to 10-year Treasury yields and 30-year mortgage rates in the United States? As studied by the Bank of England as part of its system-wide exploratory scenario, this could lead to a severe increase in long-term interest rates and justify another round of liquidity injection. It is also from this perspective that I think about the ongoing bull market in gold and silver prices.

In my view, the long-term trend for interest and mortgage rates is upward, irrespective of what the Fed does with overnight interest rates. In this context, the current downturn in the forest industry could last longer than most people think, and it could still get worse before it gets any better. Given the capital intensity of BCC's business lines and its declining liquidity position, caution is warranted here.

Conclusion

Boise Cascade is one of the largest manufacturers of wood products and distributors of building materials in North America. The company's two business lines are tightly integrated, making the portfolio more resilient during cyclical downturns relative to unintegrated peers.

While BCC's balance sheet currently looks strong with net debt of only $66.7 million, this is in the context of capital-intensive businesses generating not enough cash flows from operations to cover capital expenditures. This dynamic explains in part why the company's liquidity position deteriorated meaningfully since the start of 2025.

How long can the downturn last before the weight starts to accumulate on BCC's balance sheet? What happens to wood product demand if long-term interest rates in the United States start to rise again? Everyone will have to answer those questions for themselves, but for me, they are reminders that caution is warranted here.

data.title: Meta's Capex Plans Could Lead To Their iPhone Moment
data.description:
Introduction

For those who follow my work, you may recall that I wrote on Meta Platforms (META) once. This was back in 2018 and my very first article on Seeking Alpha. It's not that I haven’t followed it, but there is always so much noise surrounding it that it takes hours to incorporate into a full report. You may recall that when the article came out, it was post Cambridge Analytica scandal. That event seems like decades ago. Since then, there was a pandemic, and investors worried whether ad budgets would be cut to the detriment of Meta’s revenues; then there was the announcement of the metaverse and the heavy R&D spending it would require. There were fears that TikTok and Snapchat were also stealing share in digital advertising. More recently, there have been breakthroughs in AI and whether Meta could use it for improved ad targeting or whether other hyperscalers like Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) and Amazon (AMZN) would steal their market share. Meta still operated under the name Facebook at that time, and was viewed as more of a social media platform, whereas today it is viewed more as an AI infrastructure company for social networking, content sharing, and immersive technologies like augmented reality or virtual reality.

Despite all of the noise, both good and bad, this company has been one of the best compounders in public markets. This is despite a 70% drawdown in 2022. Even if you had invested at the highs in 2018 when the stock was $220/share, you would have realized a ~20% annualized return today.

I am a long-term bull of Meta, as I believe it is uniquely positioned among the hyperscalers to monetize its AI efforts the fastest, as it will augment its core businesses (advertising and messaging) and open up new revenue streams. Meta doesn’t have to hope that consumer behavior adapts to their new paradigm, unlike the hyperscalers that are building foundational models.

For example, while the cloud providers AWS, Azure, GCP may recognize quick revenue through infrastructure demand, monetization of their efforts is dependent on external workflow demand (which no doubt is high at this time). They don’t benefit from consumer engagement like Meta does, as they own the end-user relationship and capture the incremental ad spend immediately. Consumer AI leaders like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic may have exceptional frontier models but lack monetization beyond subscriptions so far.

Meta’s path to AI monetization will come through greater advertising efficiency, as tools like Advantage Plus have improved ad targeting and creative generation, leading to higher ROI for advertisers and CPMs for Meta, and content personalization, which boosts engagement through refined recommendations. There is also the messaging part, as AI can enable conversational transactions and customer service within WhatsApp and Messenger, which can create new revenue streams without building new platforms.

Meta AI may not be a widely adopted chatbot like ChatGPT or Gemini but serves niche functions within their ecosystem. Meta has a vast repository of content in the form of videos, photos, and posts. This content has the potential to enable highly visual search experiences, such as finding good restaurants, beauty or lifestyle products through user generated Reels. For example, a user could ask Meta AI for skincare products suited to their complexion and ingredient preferences and receive curated video content. This represents a differentiated opportunity to integrate AI into social discovery and commerce.

Q3 Results

Meta’s recent performance is pretty much always characterized by a tale of two quarters. First, there is the Family of Apps segment, which continues to deliver strong results. Then, there are the more speculative bets like Reality Labs, which is where the losses keep mounting. This is just a feature of investing in founder-led businesses where the founder directs funds to projects they feel passionate about without regard to the investor base. It's not a bug.

In Q3 2025, daily active users (DAUs) reached 3.5 billion. This is an 8% YoY increase and up 6% from the prior quarter. Instagram surpassed 3 billion monthly active users (MAUs) for the first time in its history. Threads continues to gain momentum with 150 million DAUs, which is almost half the size of Twitter/X. Engagement with Threads is limited as users don’t spend as much time on the app compared to Twitter, but Meta is working to improve its recommendations through its LLaMA model and other AI enhancements, reporting a 10% increase in engagement.

Time spent on core platforms is also rising, as Facebook saw a 5% increase and Instagram Video saw an increase of 30% YoY. This matters as Threads is nowhere close to full monetization potential, whereas Facebook and Instagram can immediately convert engagement to ad revenue. These trends increased revenue 26% YoY, driven by 14% ad impression growth and 10% ad price increases.

There were two notable disclosures in the earnings call that demonstrate successful AI integration that is increasing engagement. First, Reels now has a $50 billion run rate. This was previously a drag on monetization when Meta pivoted user engagement from high-margin ad formats to short-form video. Second, AI-powered tools support $60 billion in ad revenue, which demonstrates the successful adoption of the Meta Advantage Plus suite and other machine learning systems.

What must be remembered is when ad prices rise, advertisers typically see their ROI shrink because they pay more for each impression or click and could therefore reduce demand for ads in the long run. Meta’s AI tools like Advantage Plus make ads more effective and targeted, therefore improving conversion rates. Advertisers can still achieve above-average ROI with Meta’s platforms despite the higher ad costs.

Results were not all rosy , though; operating margins fell 300 bps to 40% and Family Of Apps margins dropped YoY from 54% to 49%. This reduction in operating leverage has been the result of the surge in CAPEX and R&D spending. Meta spent $15 billion on R&D this quarter alone, which was a 36% YoY increase. This increased spend is to maintain their position in consumer-facing models, as OpenAI, Google Gemini, Anthropic, and Perplexity are much more entrenched in consumer-facing generative AI. Meta’s apps hardly even make the charts as far as usage goes. Meta’s strategy is to embed AI into its platforms and open-source its LLaMA models to drive developer adoption rather than competing directly with the closed end LLMs.

Another notable expense item for the quarter was a valuation allowance adjustment under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which resulted in a $16 billion non-cash tax expense. If this charge was excluded, EPS would have been $7.25/share, or 20% higher on a YoY basis.

The largest concern is CAPEX, which management indicated could exceed $70 billion for 2025. This would be an 84% increase from 2024. Management also noted that CAPEX could exceed this figure for 2026 as well. It is beginning to look increasingly likely CAPEX spending could exceed $210 billion from 2025-2027. This is more than Meta spent in the previous seven fiscal years on CAPEX.

This significantly impacts free cash flow conversion. Despite generating ~$80 billion in operating cash flow in 2025, they only realized $29 billion in FCF. This raises critical questions for investors, such as how much of this spending is essential to support ongoing growth rather than one-time investments that will taper off.

CAPEX Risk

The way to think about CAPEX spending is whether it will be used to maintain current operations or drive growth opportunities. This distinction matters as depreciation is a cost allocation method, matching the expense of using assets to revenue generated using them. Understanding the split between maintenance and growth CAPEX highlights how much spending supports maintenance and growth CAPEX.

If META spends $70 billion in CAPEX just to maintain its current $190-$200 billion in revenue, the return profile looks very different than if this expands the revenue base.

Not only is there the risk that CAPEX outpaces operating cash flows, making FCF negligible, the depreciation lags behind CAPEX, as depreciation is realized over four to seven years. Today’s depreciation still mostly reflects allocations from periods when CAPEX was much lower (i.e., when CAPEX was $20 billion a year). The ratio of CAPEX to depreciation is currently running at ~3.6X and will create a headwind to earnings as depreciation expense catches up, as normalized depreciation is essentially four times higher than current levels. This has the potential to reduce EPS by ~$5/share on an annual basis.

I mentioned in my recent article on Google the concerns about the mismatch between the useful life of accelerators (GPUs/TPUs) and their increasing technological obsolescence in AI infrastructure. Currently, hyperscalers depreciate these assets over four to seven years, but NVIDIA’s (NVDA) product cycle continues to shorten due to massive efficiency gains. Blackwell chips deliver 10-20X more tokens per watt than a Hopper chip. The real useful life may be two to three years, which will reduce ROI and prolong the payback period on their investments. Meta, like Google and Microsoft (MSFT), is sheltered from this risk, as they can repurpose older chips for internal workloads like search, social media, video. Google is the least sensitive to this risk, as they have their homegrown TPUs, which can do some of the less inference-heavy tasks like recommendation systems.

Management has argued that this CAPEX will generate high returns similar to prior investments during the 2021-2022 period when they spent aggressively on AI to overcome Apple’s IDFA privacy changes, as these investments enabled Meta to maintain ad targeting capabilities while others like Snap (SNAP) struggled. Management argued then and does now that these investments are necessary to increase ad targeting, engagement, and measurement, and points to the increased time spent on their platforms and opportunities to serve ads. Generative AI adds another avenue for monetization by automating ad creation and improving conversion rates. It can also enable smaller advertisers to participate, which increases the numbers at auction. There is the risk that there are diminishing marginal returns on this spend, as there may be a point at which incremental compute and model improvements don’t drive engagement and ad performance enough to justify the expense. Meta’s incremental investments have delivered solid returns since 2022 (other than the metaverse), as revenue has grown $45 billion against cumulative CAPEX of $135 billion, which implies an after tax return of 16% after applying a 50% margin to incremental revenue.

Valuation Analysis

Experts see the global digital advertisement market growing ~10-15% annually over the next decade. I believe Meta can grow at the higher end of the range as a result of untapped potential in WhatsApp and Messenger.

  • Embedding AI-driven shopping experiences directly into the interface, these apps can serve as transactional hubs. A user could ask for product recommendations and receive curated options from integrated merchants, and purchase links. Meta could monetize this by charging a commission or “take rate.”
  • AI-powered customer service. Businesses already use these platforms for communication, but AI can further scale and automate these platforms. Conversational agents could replace traditional call centers and customer support firms. This is a market estimated at $500 billion in annual revenue. These services would reduce operational costs for businesses, creating new revenue streams for Meta.
  • Traditionally Google dominates high-intent ads, Meta focuses more on “discovery ads,” which are great for brand awareness but are weaker on intent. Users explicitly state preferences in conversation when messaging and therefore allow Meta to serve “search-like” ads. High-intent ads generally result in higher CPMs.

In addition, Meta should continue to boost revenues through Facebook and Instagram as follows:

  • Empowering small advertisers who often lack the budget for high-quality creatives and lack design expertise. AI tools can generate ad copy, images, and short videos based on prompts and product links, thus reducing competition in ad auctions.
  • Better conversion rates in general as a result of AI-generated creatives using more refined predictive models.
  • Reduced reliance on ad agencies. Large advertisers often allocate 30-40% of their budget to agencies for creative development. AI tools can augment or replace agencies, enabling ad campaigns to be created at scale. Savings from agency fees can be reinvested into ad spend, increasing CPMs.

Lastly, the Reality Labs smart glasses and further development of the augmented reality strategy will allow for integration with Meta’s ecosystem (like Messenger, WhatsApp, Instagram), allowing seamless messaging and content sharing, and most importantly, commerce features (i.e., in-glass shopping experiences).

Reality Labs has long been considered a “money pit” for Meta but has seen some traction with consumers with the Ray-Ban smart glasses compared to the Apple Vision Pro. Monetization opportunities include premium hardware sales, subscription tiers for more advanced AI features, AR/VR advertising, and probably more that I haven’t thought of. These investments may allow Meta to defend its market share in ad spend. The likes of Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic are beginning to show dominance in conversational and productive AI, which Meta lags in. If Meta can gain a foothold in immersive AI, this may create additional opportunities to replace what may be lost on their current platforms.

It is long forgotten that Meta faced the existential threat during the transition from desktop to mobile, but salvaged its position through acquisitions of Instagram and a rewrite of its app architecture, which at the time looked like reckless spending but now seems visionary.

I assume revenue will grow 15% for the first five years but will regress to 10% after five years. I assume EBITDA margin is in line with historical levels at ~50% for the next five years but decreases to 45% thereafter as Reality Labs becomes a greater portion of revenues. Hardware tends to have a lower margin than software.

I assume CAPEX spending at $100 billion annually for 2026-2028, which is 50% of expected revenue for 2025. This will result in very low FCF conversion, but CAPEX will revert to normal levels (20% of revenue) after 2029. I assume terminal growth at 3.5% and a WACC of 8.5%.

The most notable thing from this analysis is when we take a longer-term time horizon (10 years), and when revenue growth is maintained at 10-15%, the increased CAPEX spend over the first three years only makes a difference of ~$48/share in intrinsic value relative to if CAPEX stayed at its normalized levels. This dynamic mirrors past concerns about Reality Labs, which burns over $20 billion annually, yet Meta still realizes $90-$100 billion in annual operating cash flow anyway. DCF analysis clearly shows that the large CAPEX spend is more than justified if Meta can just maintain its current ROIC over the next decade, as my DCF analysis projects a fair value of $688/share even with elevated CAPEX spend.

DCF analysis projects significant upside potential with the current share price of ~$610/share.

Currently Meta trades at the lowest valuation of the major hyperscalers at 19X expected 2025 earnings. I would argue Meta has the highest upside case out of all of the major hyperscalers but also the highest risk profile. Meta has the ability to scale faster through its existing businesses, creating a shorter payback period on its CAPEX spend. However, execution is paramount given there will inevitably be regulatory hurdles, as Meta is always at the center of privacy and antitrust scrutiny, which may constrain personalization and commerce initiatives. In addition, more than 90% of Meta’s revenue is ad spend, where there is becoming increasingly more competition from the likes of Amazon and Google, which may reduce Meta's ROI. Not only that, but advertising is a highly cyclical business, and one of the first things cut after a downtrend in the business cycle is the marketing budget. The other hyperscalers are a bit more sheltered from these risks.

Conclusion

Meta is one of the most compelling yet complex stories in technology. Its ability to integrate AI into core platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp positions it uniquely to monetize innovation faster than its hyperscaler peers, as it can leverage its direct consumer relationships in its advertising ecosystem.

There are near-term risks such as soaring CAPEX, margin compression, and increased competition for ad spend dollars that cannot be ignored, but the long-term thesis is supported by strong engagement trends and emerging opportunities in AI ad efficiency, messaging, and immersive technologies.

Execution will be critical to navigate regulatory hurdles and increased competition for ad spend. Mark Zuckerberg controls ~60% of voting rights through Class B shares, which carry 10 votes each, and it may appear as though the corporate structure prioritizes “founder vision” at the expense of shareholder democracy; by the same token, Zuckerberg owns a 14% economic interest, which is among the highest of large-cap tech companies. Although the structure may incentivize bold bets, it also incentivizes a long-term vision, which, to this point, Meta has executed on.

Long-term investors have continually been rewarded on this stock despite enduring significant ups and downs. Meta will likely continue to have this risk profile, but patient investors could find they are holding the company that redefines advertising and social commerce in the AI era.

data.title: Kyverna Therapeutics: Immune Reset Is Clinically Validated, Market Ignores Potential
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Holger Kujath as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

The Immune Reset Hypothesis

Kyverna Therapeutics (KYTX) just released data that could fundamentally de-risk its entire 'immune reset' platform. On October 29, interim Phase 2 data for its CAR-T therapy, KYV-101, showed revolutionary efficacy in severe myasthenia gravis (MG). The therapy didn't just meet endpoints; it doubled the efficacy of the current standard of care.

In my view, the market has failed to price in this de-risking. At a current price of ~$7, Kyverna appears decoupled from this clinical success, offering a highly asymmetric risk-reward profile. I am initiating coverage with a Strong Buy and a $55 price target, based on a risk-adjusted valuation that, in light of this data, may still be conservative.

To understand why this is a big shift, investors must first understand the devastating nature of MG. It is an autoimmune disease where autoantibodies block the communication between nerves and muscles, leading to profound, life-altering weakness. This can impair vision, swallowing, speaking, and even breathing.

The new therapy is a living drug designed to reboot the patient's faulty immune system. In autoimmune disease, the body's B-cells have gone rogue, producing antibodies that attack the body itself. The process involves:

  • Harvesting: The patient's own T-cells (the soldiers of the immune system) are collected.

  • Reprogramming: In a lab, these T-cells are engineered with a Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR) that trains them to hunt a specific target: the CD19 protein, a marker found on all B-cells (they produce the antibodies that trigger the autoimmune disease).

  • Reinfusion: The newly trained T-cells are returned to the patient, where they seek out and eliminate the entire B-cell population and therefore shut down the antibody factories.

This deep depletion creates the reset. The body's bone marrow, which is untouched, then begins to build a fresh, naive B-cell population, ideally one that has forgotten how to produce the problematic antibodies.

The clinical validation of this therapy is a paradigm shift. For years, the promise of a 'one-and-done' CAR-T intervention for autoimmune disease (a true 'immune reset') has been weighed down by toxicity fears. Kyverna's data, which combined unparalleled efficacy with a strong safety profile (0 high-grade CRS, 0 ICANS), has clinically validated the entire concept.

This clinical validation is a paradigm shift. For years, the promise of a one-and-done CAR-T intervention for autoimmune disease has been weighed down by toxicity fears. These concerns are not hypothetical; they are well-documented hurdles based on the oncology experience. In cancer treatment, severe side effects like Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) and Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome (ICANS) are known, challenging risks. Studies have documented severe, life-threatening neurotoxicity in up to 31% of patients receiving certain anti-CD19 CAR-T cells, with some cases proving fatal.

Kyverna's data, which combined unparalleled efficacy with a strong safety profile (0 high-grade CRS, 0 ICANS), has now clinically validated the entire concept.

The Scientific Basis: Differentiation of KYV-101

The goal of CAR-T in autoimmunity is the deep and rapid depletion of the pathogenic B-cell line, as the factories of harmful antibodies. The construction of the chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) is deterministic for the balance between efficacy and safety.

KYV-101 is an autologous (cells taken from the body) CD19 antigen CAR-T therapy based on a construct licensed by the National Institutes of Health (NIH).2 It contains two central differentiating features:

  • Fully Human Binder: KYV-101 uses a fully human binder. This is designed to reduce immunogenicity compared to older mouse-based binders, potentially ensuring the persistence of CAR-T cells and the durability of the response.
  • CD28 costimulation domain: KYV-101 uses a CD28 domain that induces rapid, potent T cell expansion – the necessary "firepower" for rapid B cell depletion.

Historically, CD28 domains have been associated with higher toxicity than other domains used by competitors such as Cabaletta Bio (CABA). The central scientific debate focused on this trade-off between potency and safety. However, the specific design implemented in KYV-101 appears to achieve potency while preventing toxicity. The available clinical data suggest that Kyverna has found an excellent balance.

Clinical Validation: A Paradigm Shift in Myasthenia Gravis

My thesis is based on the interim data from the Phase 2 KYSA-6 study (N=6). An understanding of the patient population is essential for this. These were patients with severe disease and a high burden of disease (mean MG-ADL score of 11.2, which measures limitations caused by MG in everyday life), in whom other therapies have not yet worked. As Dr. Srikanth Muppidi of Stanford Medicine pointed out, this baseline value is higher than in comparable, published phase 3 studies. This underlines that KYV-101 was evaluated in a population that is difficult to treat.

Strong effectiveness

At the primary endpoint at 24 weeks, KYV-101 achieved a mean reduction of -8.0 points on the MG-ADL scale. Since the MG-ADL scale quantifies the limitations in everyday life, an improvement of this magnitude is transformative. It implies that patients who were previously suffering from severe muscle weakness, leaving them highly impaired, were rendered almost symptom-free after treatment. Take a look at the following results provided by latest Kyverna presentation.

The current standard of care (SoC), including high-priced biologics such as FcRn blockers (e.g., Argenx's Vyvgart) and C5 inhibitors, typically achieves reductions in the range of -3 to -4 points when administered regularly. KYV-101 has thus doubled the efficacy benchmark.

The efficacy of KYV-101 was not just profound, it was consistent and rapid:

  • 100% response rate: All six patients achieved clinically meaningful improvement (defined as ≥2 points on the MG-ADL).5
  • Rapid onset of action: A substantial mean reduction of -7.8 points was measured as early as two weeks after infusion.
  • Deep reactions: A significant proportion of patients achieved Minimal Symptom Expression (MSE) (MG-ADL 0-1), which means a return almost symptom-free.

The validated "immune reset"

Perhaps the most transformative aspect of the data lies in that of the "one-and-done" hypothesis. By week 24, 100% of patients were able to discontinue all background medications (steroids, immunosuppressants, and biologics). The actual goal of therapy was achieved: the transition from chronic management to a real, drug-free life.

The data also supports the 'one-and-done' hypothesis. Five of the six patients remained completely drug-free at their last follow-up (up to 36 weeks). One patient resumed medication after week 33. However, this incident did not change the evaluation of the study, CMO Dr. Naji Gehchan provided context:

"This restart was a patient-initiated decision and was not due to a disease flare or relapse. The patient remained in Minimal Symptom Expression (MSE)." - Dr. Naji Gehchan, CMO

A Clean Safety Profile: Overcoming the Toxicity Barrier

The previous limitation for the widespread use of CAR-T in non-oncological settings was the risk of severe toxicity. The KYSA-6 data show that this risk was significantly reduced.

The safety profile was convincing, with 0 cases of high-grade (grade ≥3) CRS (cytokine storm) and 0 cases of ICANS (neurotoxicity) of any degree.

These safety data are consistent across more than 100 other patients treated with KYV-101 in various indications. Data from rheumatoid arthritis studies, for example, also showed no cases of severe CRS or ICANS.6

This leaves the potential for outpatient administration, which would significantly improve commercial viability and patient access. Kyverna appears to have achieved best-in-class safety with a highly effective therapy.

Pipeline expansion and catalysts

The implications of the MG data validate the mechanism of action and reduce the risk of the entire KYV-101 franchise.

Phase 3 Significantly De-Risked: The upcoming pivotal study for MG is significantly lower risk. The exceptional effect size in phase 2 (-8.0 points) clearly exceeds the statistical assumptions for the phase 3 superiority study. This significantly increases the probability of success. The start of the phase 3 study (≈60 patients) is scheduled for the end of 2025.

SPS (Stiff-Person-Syndrome) is a devastating, rare neurological disorder where autoantibodies attack the central nervous system. This causes extreme muscle rigidity. Patients are often described as living statues. With no approved disease-modifying therapies, SPS represents a unmet need and a faster potential path to BLA filing. It serves as the perfect spearhead and allows Kyverna to validate its complex CAR-T manufacturing and logistics in a defined, niche market before scaling up for the far larger MG opportunity

The management premium and financial stability

In the complex sector of cell therapy, the risk of execution, especially manufacturing and logistics, is of great importance. Kyverna has a strategic advantage here. CEO Warner Biddle (former Global Head of Commercial at Kite Pharma) and Board Member Christi Shaw (former CEO of Kite Pharma) bring valuable expertise from Kite Pharma (acquired by Gilead). They were instrumental in the successful commercialization of Yescarta. This "kite DNA" confirms competence in scaling complex therapies, which significantly reduces the risk of execution compared to typical clinical biotech companies.

Financially, Kyverna is solidly positioned. The company has $211.7 million in cash and cash equivalents as of June 30, 2025, which ensures a liquidity reserve until 2027, according to the latest 10-Q filing.9 This eliminates near-term funding pressures and secures funding for the Phase 3 MG study and preparation for the SPS launch.

Evaluation: Calculation for the MG and SPS indication

In my opinion, the current valuation significantly underestimates the potential of KYV-101. I use a risk-adjusted Net Present Value model, focused on the MG results.

The initial target market includes approximately 12,000 refractory patients in the U.S. who have not responded to at least one established therapy. This serves as a starting point for a broader U.S. market, estimated at 40,000 patients.

This figure is a conservative estimate supported by top-down data. Independent studies place the total prevalence of Myasthenia Gravis in the U.S. between ~82,700 and ~116,300 patients. Crucially, medical literature indicates that approximately 10-15% of these patients are considered treatment-refractory.

The 12,000 patient figure used in the model falls directly within this externally validated 10-15% corridor. This serves as a starting point for a broader U.S. market, estimated at 40,000 patients.

Proposed Revision (To Address Feedback)

Valuation: My DCF Model

In my view, the current valuation fails to price in the fundamental de-risking from the Phase 2 data. My $55 price target is derived from a Sum-of-the-Parts (SOTP) analysis for the Myasthenia Gravis and indication plus SPS.

Key Operational Assumptions

I use standard industry benchmarks but make a key adjustment for Cost of Goods Sold (COGS). The COGS for CAR-T is structurally high due to its personalized manufacturing process. My 35% assumption is aggressive, but I believe it's justified by the management's Kite DNA and proven expertise in scaling CAR-T production.

Parameter Assumption Rationale
COGS (% of Sales) 35%

High, reflecting CAR-T complexity, but assumes "Kite DNA" efficiency.
SG&A (% of Sales) 25%

Standard benchmark for specialized commercial launch.
R&D (% of Sales) 10%

Post-approval costs.
Tax Rate 21%

U.S. corporate rate.

Illustrative Peak Free Cash Flow Calculation

These assumptions produce a clear cash flow calculation, shown below for the illustrative peak sales year ($1.05B).

Metric Calculation Value (Peak Year)
Peak Sales ($350k * 3,000 patients) $1,050 M
(-) COGS (35% of Sales) ($368 M)
(-) SG&A (25% of Sales) ($263 M)
(-) R&D (10% of Sales) ($105 M)
EBIT $314 M
(-) Taxes (21%) ($66 M)
Free Cash Flow $248 M

Price Target

Discounting this FCF stream at the 12% WACC and applying the data-justified 65% Probability of Success yields the $2.5 billion rNPV (risk-adjusted Net Present Value) for the MG indication.

To remain cautious and consider the potential need for future financing for commercialization (despite the reserve until 2027), I model moderate future dilution, resulting in an estimated total share count of approximately 58 million.

Component Value
rNPV (Myasthenia Gravis) $2,500 M
rNPV (Stiff Person Synd.) $500 M
Net Cash (June 30, '25) $211.7 M
Total Equity Value $3,211.7 M
Price Target (per 58M shares) ~$55

The Bear Case: Risk Analysis

A strong buy thesis for a clinical-stage company requires an analysis of the uncertainty factors.

Durability and sample size: The key questions

The greatest risk lies in the extrapolation of the available data.

  • Small sample size (N=6): The KYSA-6 data, while exceptional, are based on only six patients. These results need to be replicated in the larger Phase 3 study (N=60). It is possible that the strong results are a statistical outlier or that severe side effects occur in the larger sample.
  • Durability of response: Current follow-up lasts up to 36 weeks. The value proposition of a "one-and-done" therapy depends on several years of remission. The risk of relapse after several years can only be evaluated by long-term data.
  • Long-term safety: Risks such as secondary malignancies (a class-wide risk in CAR-T) or severe infections due to prolonged B-cell aplasia require long-term monitoring.

The Competitive Landscape

The field of autoimmune CAR-T therapies is developing rapidly.

  • Direct CAR-T Competition: Cabaletta Bio is developing CABA-201 (4-1BB domain).11 The scientific debate between CD28 (KYV-101) and 4-1BB regarding the optimal balance of potency, persistence, and safety is ongoing, although the safety profile of KYV-101 currently appears favorable. Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) is also active in this area.12
  • RNAC is pursuing an outpatient, chemo-free approach with Descartes08 (mRNACART vs. BCMA) and is already in the approval setting with the pivotal phase3 AURORA STUDY (FDASPA, RMAT). The therapy includes six weekly infusions without lymphodepletion.

Enterprise

Candidate

Construct/Domain

Differentiation

Status

KYV-101

Complete. Human CD19/CD28

Potent/Fast Reset; Clean Security (0 ICANS)

Potential for longer persistence

Proprietary

Large pharmaceutical infrastructure

mRNA-CAR-T, anti-BCMA; without lymphodepletion

Outpatient; 6× weekly infusions; no CRS/ICANS so far; Signals for lasting effect

Phase 3 (AURORA, SPA/RMAT), recruitment on track
  • Established standard of care: The current SoC (FcRn blockers, C5 inhibitors) consists of well-established chronic therapies. KYV-101 must demonstrate a clear, lasting benefit to motivate physicians and patients to accept the intense CAR-T process.

Operational risks

  • Manufacturing Complexity (CMC): CAR-T manufacturing is complex and cost-intensive. Scaling production and reducing COGS are critical factors. Although management's "Kite Pharma DNA" mitigates this risk, it does not eliminate it completely.
  • Payer acceptance: Securing adequate reimbursement for a high-priced ($350k+) one-time treatment in a non-oncology setting is challenging. Cost bearers could implement restrictive criteria to control the high initial costs.

Conclusion: A Highly Asymmetric Opportunity

The Phase 2 data for KYV-101 in Myasthenia Gravis represent a revolution for the treatment of this serious autoimmune disease and for the company itself. Doubling the efficacy compared to the standard of care while achieving a drug-free remission in 100% of refractory patients makes a paradigm shift possible.

Crucially, this was achieved with a first-class safety profile, which addresses the central barrier to CAR-T adoption and validates Kyverna's specific therapy.

Although risks remain in terms of long-term effect and small sample size, the success of the clinical effect significantly reduces the risk of the upcoming Phase 3 trial. With a defined path to market (SPS BLA 1H 2026), experienced management of Kite Pharma and a strong balance sheet, Kyverna is well positioned to realize a multi-billion-dollar opportunity.

My rNPV analysis supports a price target of $55. I conclude that the current valuation reflects an outdated skepticism and offers a highly asymmetric risk-reward profile. For this reason, I am long KYTX myself.

data.title: The Fed Cuts, But Confidence Wavers: Our Outlook
data.description:
The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) delivered a widely expected 25 basis point rate cut at its October meeting, marking the second consecutive reduction and bringing the target range to 3.75-4.00%.

However, expectations for further easing quickly faded after Chairman Jerome Powell cautioned that a December rate cut was “not a foregone conclusion – far from it.” Before the meeting, markets had effectively priced another cut in December as inevitable, but odds have since dropped to near even, injecting fresh uncertainty into the final FOMC meeting of 2025.

For investors, this shifting outlook reinforces the importance of maintaining flexibility in fixed income positioning. Duration exposure has become more sensitive to data releases and Fed communications, making active management essential in the weeks ahead.

The Fed continues to navigate a challenging backdrop: a steadily cooling labor market alongside inflation that remains stubbornly above its long-run target. Progress on price stability has stalled in recent quarters as core goods inflation rises, influenced by new trade tariffs, a weaker US dollar, and improving global growth.

Still, most FOMC members appear increasingly concerned about labor market softness. Job openings have declined steadily, unemployment hovers near 4.3%, and private sector hiring outside of healthcare and education has been negative for two straight quarters. Moderating wage growth may help restrain services inflation and support the case for further policy easing.

For investors, this mixed data underscores the value of diversifying sources of income, which could include short-duration bonds, multi-sector credit, and selective alternatives that can perform across varying rate environments.

The recent government shutdown – the longest on record – has complicated the Fed’s assessment by limiting access to official data. Private indicators show a continued but gradual slowdown in employment, with notable headlines on layoffs at major companies.

Given still loose financial conditions, the case for additional easing remains ambiguous. There is a meaningful chance the Fed pauses and leaves rates unchanged in December.

2026 Thoughts

Looking beyond year-end, markets anticipate the policy rate could move toward 3.0% by the end of 2026, while the Fed’s own projections remain slightly more cautious, with a median forecast around 3.4%.

A terminal rate in the low 3% range appears achievable if labor markets continue to weaken. One emerging source of uncertainty is the leadership transition expected when Chair Powell’s term expires in mid-2026, as political pressure builds for a more dovish successor.

The MOVE Index, which tracks implied volatility in US rates markets, now sits near its three-year low. Investors should consider maintaining exposure to the tails of the distribution, as complacency may prove costly in a year shaped by policy uncertainty and shifting macro dynamics.

We can say with conviction entering 2026 that volatility – not just in interest rates but across all asset classes – will define the year ahead. Despite historically low implied volatility across rates, FX, credit, and equities, the potential for sharp dislocations is rising.

data.title: Tradeweb Exchange-Traded Funds Update - October 2025
data.description:
The following data is derived from trading activity on the Tradeweb Markets institutional European- and U.S.-listed ETF platforms.

European-Listed ETFs

Total traded volume

Trading activity on the Tradeweb European ETF marketplace amounted to EUR 72.8 billion in October. The percentage of transactions and notional volume completed via Tradeweb’s Automated Intelligent Execution (AiEX) tool registered at 94% and 30%, respectively.

Adam Gould, Global Head of Equities at Tradeweb, said, “Our European ETF platform delivered another strong performance in October, with the combination of broad client-base growth, increased ETF adoption and our automated rules-based RFQ trading protocol driving deeper liquidity and greater efficiency for the market.”

Volume breakdown

In October, equities remained the most actively traded ETF asset class, comprising 63% of the overall platform flow. In commodity-based funds, ‘sells’ exceeded ‘buys’ by 42 percentage points, whereas equity- and fixed income-based products saw net buying by 18 and 8 percentage points, respectively.

North America Equities reclaimed its spot as the most heavily traded ETF category during the month, generating over EUR 15 billion in monthly volume, after Global Equities briefly topped the table in September.

Top ten by traded notional volume

In October, the two most actively traded ETFs were commodity-based, with the remainder consisting of equity- and fixed income-based funds. The Invesco Physical Gold ETC and the iShares Physical Gold ETC, which both offer exposure to the performance of the gold spot price, placed first and second, while the iShares Core S&P 500 UCITS ETF came in third.

U.S.-Listed ETFs

Total traded volume

Total consolidated U.S. ETF notional value traded in October reached USD 91.3 billion.

Adam Gould, Global Head of Equities at Tradeweb, said, “October was the third best-performing month on record for our U.S. institutional ETF platform, with volumes increasing by 59.6% year-over-year, as clients increasingly rely on streamlined, scalable execution. Meanwhile, the percentage of transactions executed via AiEX reached an all-time high of 51%.”

Volume breakdown

As a percentage of total notional value, equities accounted for 54% and fixed income for 39%, with the remainder comprising commodity and specialty ETFs.

Top ten by traded notional volume

In a reversal of September’s trend, seven of the top ten ETFs by traded notional volume in October were fixed income-based, with the remainder being equity-based.

The iShares US Treasury Bond ETF, which last appeared in the top three in March 2024, placed first. Meanwhile, the JP Morgan BetaBuilders Europe ETF and Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF came in second and third place, respectively.

data.title: Chinese Data Shows Momentum Fading Into The Final Stretch Of The Year
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

Investment continues to disappoint amid downbeat forecasts as corporates stay sidelined

Chinese fixed asset investment slumped further to -1.7% year-over-year, year-to-date, marking the lowest level since June 2020. Once again, the data is falling well short of already downbeat forecasts.

By category, we saw infrastructure investment fall into contraction territory at -0.1% YoY ytd, contracting for the first time since 2020. Manufacturing investment, which had been a solid outperformer for several years, slumped to just 2.7% YoY ytd. Private sector investment fell to -4.5% YoY ytd, while even government investment slowed to 0.1% YoY ytd and looks likely to tip into contraction territory next month.

The only silver linings were seen in China's recent growth areas, namely the auto (17.5%) and rail, ships, and aeroplanes (20.1%) categories.

While the magnitude of the declines was somewhat surprising, the continued weakness of investment itself was not. Anti-involution policies targeting excessive price competition and the shift toward reducing redundant investments could be adding to the downward pressure. Yesterday's poor credit data showed new RMB loans actually contracted by RMB 20.1bn in October. While October tends to be a seasonally weak month due to the Golden Week holidays, the lack of borrowing demand has been quite clear year-to-date, with new RMB loans down 7.4% year-to-date. All this suggests real interest rates remain too high to attract prospective borrowers.

FAI is slowing across the board

Property pressures may persist absent new support

October's 70 city property prices continued to show downward pressure. New home prices fell by -0.45% month-on-month, the steepest decline since October 2024. Used home prices fell by -0.66% MoM, the steepest decline since September 2024. After flatlining in the first quarter, prices have fallen at a faster pace. From the peak, new home prices are now down -11.8%, while used home prices are down -20.3%. Of the 70 cities, 45 have seen secondary market prices decline between 20% and 30% from their peak, while 3 have experienced price drops of more than 30% from the peak.

In the primary market, 6 of 70 cities saw prices stabilise or increase, the lowest proportion since September 2024. In the secondary market, we saw a second consecutive month without a single city seeing stable or rising price levels.

With falling prices and still elevated inventory levels, it is no surprise to see that property investment remains one of the biggest drags on the economy, with property investment now down -14.7% YoY ytd.

The recent downturn, with accelerating price declines, has yet to be adequately addressed. More policy support is needed to prevent the massive destruction of household wealth, which would significantly hinder efforts to transition toward a consumption-driven growth model.

Secondary market prices have mostly fallen 20% or more from the peak

Retail sales moderated by less than expected

October's retail sales data slowed to 2.9% year-on-year, down only 0.1pp from September's reading. That’s a little stronger than market expectations. Year-to-date, retail sales are up 4.3% YoY and should still be able to close 2025 stronger than last year's 3.5% growth. But the loss of momentum in the second half of the year nonetheless remains a little disappointing given the stated importance of domestic demand.

The major outperformer in October was seen in gold and jewellery sales, which rose to 37.6% YoY, the highest level since April 2023, as retail buying picked up with gold reaching its all-time high in October.

As we discussed in the past few months of reports, the impact of the trade-in policy continues to fade. Key beneficiary categories, including household appliances, communication appliances, and furniture, have been on a downward trend. In particular, we saw household appliance sales plunge -14.6% YoY in October, as similar promotions in 2024 create an unfavourable base effect. This pattern has previously been seen in auto sales, one of the earliest beneficiaries of the trade-in policy. A similar drag will likely emerge for communication appliances early next year.

While the policy has been successful in frontloading consumption this year, a new policy direction will likely be needed to support consumption next year.

Fading trade-in policy impact suggests a new direction for consumption support may be needed

Industrial production slowdown to be monitored closely

October value added of industry growth slowed to 4.9% YoY, down from 6.5%. This came in below expectations for a smaller decline, marking the lowest growth since August 2024. The year-to-date growth edged down 0.1pp to 6.1% YoY as a result.

By industry, we continue to see the same themes outperform. Auto manufacturing (16.8%) and rail, ships, and aeroplane manufacturing (15.2%) remain clear outperformers. We’re still seeing strong growth in semiconductor (17.7%), industrial robot (17.9%), and service robot (12.8%) production. Advanced manufacturing was identified as one of the top priorities for China in the next Five-Year Plan and is expected to continue experiencing strong growth and investment.

Industrial production has been a key driver of growth, with manufacturing meeting external demand this year. Soft industrial production confirmed the weakness we saw earlier in the month with the steeper-than-expected downturn of the purchasing managers' index. While one month of soft data does not necessarily indicate a trend, the next few months appear to have a less supportive base effect. The deceleration of industrial production should be watched closely along with exports. Signs that external demand and manufacturing are slowing will represent significant risks to the overall outlook, given the softness of domestic demand.

Industrial production slowed in October

Slowdown suggests that policy support direction won't change

Following the continued softness of the October data and an uptick in inflation, which could reduce the tailwind from the GDP deflator, risks to our 2025 GDP growth forecast of 5.0% appear somewhat balanced to the downside heading into the final stretch of the year.

There’s reduced urgency for new policy support. Stronger-than-expected growth in the first three quarters of the year is likely keeping the economy on track to meet its 2025 growth target without requiring much additional intervention. With ambitious targets set by Premier Li for 2030, targeting a GDP of RMB 170tn, along with President Xi's aim from 2020 to double growth by 2035, it will be a marathon, not a sprint.

As such, the recent lull in new stimulus is likely only a pause rather than a reversal of direction. Policymakers may opt to conserve ammunition for next year's growth targets. We still see room for further monetary easing next year, and fiscal support for consumption and strategic industries will likely continue.

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
